articles.title: The Secret Behind 80-Year-Olds Who Remember Like They're 50
articles.summary: What if you could be 80 and still ace a memory test like a 50-year-old? That's exactly what 'SuperAgers' do—and after 25 years of research, scientists finally know why. Northwestern University studied 290 SuperAgers and 77 donated brains. They discovered two paths to super-aging: 'resistance' (brains that never develop harmful amyloid and tau proteins) and 'resilience' (brains that have those Alzheimer's-linked proteins but somehow stay sharp). SuperAgers also have larger, healthier neurons in memory regions and tend to be highly social. This groundbreaking study, published in Alzheimer's & Dementia, proves that cognitive decline isn't inevitable—and could lead to new ways to protect everyone's brain.

articles.title: The Great Fat Debate: Why the New Food Pyramid Has Scientists Fuming
articles.summary: The U.S. government just unveiled a new food pyramid that puts butter, beef tallow, and olive oil at the top—suggesting we should eat lots of them. But nutrition experts are pushing back hard. They say swapping animal fats (saturated fats like butter) for plant-based fats (unsaturated fats like olive and seed oils) is proven to lower the risk of heart disease and diabetes. The problem? The new guidelines were based on a report from a group with ties to the meat and dairy industry, not the usual scientific advisory committee. Experts worry that pushing animal fats could lead people to eat less fiber from fruits, veggies, and grains. So what's the real science? Nuts, seeds, fish, and olive oil are…

articles.title: Trees Glow With Electricity During Storms—And Scientists Just Caught It on Camera
articles.summary: For over 70 years, scientists theorized that trees might emit tiny electrical sparks called 'corona discharges' during thunderstorms—but no one had ever confirmed it in nature. Until now. A team from Penn State drove a modified van with a custom telescope to North Carolina and captured 859 corona events on a sweetgum tree during a two-hour storm. These faint UV glows, caused by electrical imbalances between storm clouds and the ground, also produce hydroxyl—a chemical that helps clean the air by breaking down pollutants and methane. Published in Geophysical Research Letters, this is the first field confirmation of a phenomenon long suspected but never seen. 'There's still discovery science being done,' said lead author Patrick McFarland.
